sentence: Identify non-fluoropolymer thermoplastics that can withstand severe thermal shock, specifically direct immersion into liquid nitrogen (−196 °C) and maintain structural integrity (no cracking, embrittlement, or catastrophic failure). Please note that silicone doesn't account for thermoplastics. The identified material can be formulated to allow extrusion into films and injection molding components.

required: Key material attributes, performance goals, constraints and critical PEEK‑benchmarks for a non‑fluoropolymer cryogenic thermoplastic
Chemical resistance ≥ PEEK (water, steam, inert gases)
Low VOC emissions (≤ X ppm, per ISO 16000‑2)
Injectable into complex molds (≤ 10 min cycle)
High thermal shock resistance (≤ 5 % stress rise)
Low coefficient of thermal expansion at cryogenic range
High barrier to gas diffusion (≤ 1 × 10⁻⁶ cc / s)
Excellent surface finish for optical/medical use

extracted_keywords: Key material attributes, performance goals, constraints and critical PEEK‑benchmarks for a non‑fluoropolymer cryogenic thermoplastic
Chemical resistance ≥ PEEK (water, steam, inert gases)
Low VOC emissions (≤ X ppm, per ISO 16000‑2)
Injectable into complex molds (≤ 10 min cycle)
High thermal shock resistance (≤ 5 % stress rise)
Low coefficient of thermal expansion at cryogenic range
High barrier to gas diffusion (≤ 1 × 10⁻⁶ cc / s)
Excellent surface finish for optical/medical use
extracted_constraints: Must be PFAS‑free – the material cannot contain any per‑ or poly‑fluoroalkyl substances.
Must survive direct immersion in liquid nitrogen (−196 °C) without cracking, embrittlement, or catastrophic failure – the polymer must retain its structural integrity at that temperature.
Must exhibit water absorption ≤ 0.15 % by weight (24 h) – any higher moisture uptake disqualifies the material.
Must have a coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) equal to or lower than that of PEEK (≈ 30–36 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) – higher CTE values would cause unacceptable dimensional changes upon cooling.
Must possess a glass‑transition temperature (T_g) well above −196 °C – a Tg near or below this temperature would result in loss of stiffness.
Must meet or exceed PEEK’s mechanical strength benchmarks:
(any lower values render the material unsuitable).
Must remain dimensionally stable (minimal creep/relaxation) at cryogenic temperatures – significant creep would lead to dimensional failure.
Must resist degradation or out‑gassing in the cryogenic environment – any evidence of chemical breakdown or gas release at −196 °C disqualifies the material.
Must be chemically compatible with inert gases, residual gases, and any cryogenic lubricants used in the application – resistance to these substances is mandatory.
